coredata.dc:title: Rationally designed smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensing platform based on B, N-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric detection of propyl gallate
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiangbin
Yang, Wei
Liu, Yuanfa
coredata.dc:description: The development of rapid and sensitive detection methods for propyl Gallate (PG) is crucial due to its significant threats to human health and the environmental safety. Here, B, N co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were designed by employing 5-boronoisophthal and o-phenylenediamine as precursors to enable fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode detecting of PG. Specifically, the presence of PG could result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity, while simultaneously leading to the formation of a distinctive tawny complex that exhibited a pronounced colorimetric response. The fluorimetric and colorimetric mode demonstrated dependable selectivity and exceptional sensitivity, with detection limits of 0.12 μg/mL and 0.27 μg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence sensing mechanism was confirmed to be attributed to the static quenching effect (SQE) through both experimental results and density functional theory (DFT) analysis, indicating that the "ON-OFF" progress could be ascribed to the formation of a non-luminescent substance. Additionally, the application of actual samples detection demonstrated outstanding self-calibration capabilities and achieved satisfactory recovery rats ranging from 94.0 % to 112.0 %. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensing system exhibited the capability to conveniently and visually assess PG levels by accurately distinguish subtle variations in color. The study presents an effective dual-mode sensing approach for rapid and accurate detection of PG, which offers significant benefits in reducing the probability of PG exposure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Propyl gallate
Dual-mode probe
DFT calculation
Smartphone-assisted device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of PG based on smartphone-sensing platform
Application for detection in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characteristics of the prepared BN-CDs. (a) TEM images of BN-CDs (Inset: the high-resolution TEM image). (b) Particle size distribution of BN-CDs. (c) XRD pattern of BN-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of BN-CDs. (e) XPS full spectrum of BN-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of (f) C 1s, (g) O 1s and (h) B 1s of BN-CDs.

Optical properties and analytical performance of BN-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence profiles of BN-CDs (Inset: the photos under natural and UV light). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of BN-CDs under different excitation signals. (c) The emission spectra of BN-CDs before and after addition PG (Inset: the photos under UV light). (d) The UV–vis signal at 450 nm of BN-CDs before and after addition PG (Inset: the photos under natural light).

Detection performances of the constructed dual-mode probe. (a) Fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs probe adding various concentrations of PG (0–150 μg/mL). (b) The related measure data (Inset: the corresponding fluorescence images at different concentrations of PG (λex = 365 nm)). (c) The corresponding linear fitting curve between I530 and lnCPG. (d) UV–vis absoprtion spectra of BN-CDs probe adding various concentrations of ACR (0.5–100 μg/mL). (e) The related measure data (Inset: the corresponding colorimetry images at different concentrations of PG). (f) The corresponding linear fitting curve between A450 and lnCPG.

Selectivity and anti-interference properties of the dual-mode probe. (a, b) Selectivity study of dual-mode probe for recognizing PG and its structural analogues. (c, d) Anti-interference study of the dual-mode probe against various metal ions.

Sensing mechanism research. (a) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of BN-CDs before and after adding PG. (b) Stern-Volmer plots for the BN-CDs sensing system at three different temperatures. (c) Calculated HOMO/LUMO distribution of BN-CDs before and after adding PG. (d) Working mechanism of BN-CDs for PG sensing.

Portable smartphone-based sensor for PG detection. (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation and operation of this sensing platform. (b) The standard curve between lnCPG and R/(G + B) obtained through smartphone visualization under fluorescent mode (Inset: optical photos of test plate under UV lamp). (c) The standard curve between lnCPG and R/(G + B) obtained through smartphone visualization under colorimetric mode (Inset: optical photos of test plate under visible light).
Schematic illustration showing the synthesis BN-CDs and its applications for the dual-mode detection.
The determination of PG spiked in real samples using dual-mode probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rationally designed smartphone-assisted dual-mode sensing platform based on B, N-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric detection of propyl gallate

National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food

Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of rapid and sensitive detection methods for propyl Gallate (PG) is crucial due to its significant threats to human health and the environmental safety. Here, B, N co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were designed by employing 5-boronoisophthal and o-phenylenediamine as precursors to enable fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode detecting of PG. Specifically, the presence of PG could result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity, while simultaneously leading to the formation of a distinctive tawny complex that exhibited a pronounced colorimetric response. The fluorimetric and colorimetric mode demonstrated dependable selectivity and exceptional sensitivity, with detection limits of 0.12 μg/mL and 0.27 μg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence sensing mechanism was confirmed to be attributed to the static quenching effect (SQE) through both experimental results and density functional theory (DFT) analysis, indicating that the "ON-OFF" progress could be ascribed to the formation of a non-luminescent substance. Additionally, the application of actual samples detection demonstrated outstanding self-calibration capabilities and achieved satisfactory recovery rats ranging from 94.0 % to 112.0 %. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensing system exhibited the capability to conveniently and visually assess PG levels by accurately distinguish subtle variations in color. The study presents an effective dual-mode sensing approach for rapid and accurate detection of PG, which offers significant benefits in reducing the probability of PG exposure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic phenolic antioxidants are commonly employed as additives to mitigate oxidative reactions in the fields of industrial manufacturing and food processing [1]. Propyl Gallate (PG), also referred to as n-propyl 3,4,5-trihydroxybenzoate, is widely favored for its exceptional thermal stability and potent antioxidant properties [2]. However, the excessive presence of PG in both food and the environment has been previously indicated to potentially pose risks to human health, including respiratory irritation, hepatic dysfunction, allergic reactions, and testicular toxicity [3,4]. As a result, numerous countries such as the United States, New Zealand, and China have implemented strict regulations on the permissible levels of PG found in processed food. The permissible limit of PG in food products is set at 100 mg kg−1 in China [5]. Moreover, the Food Additives Department of the FAO and World Health Organization recommends an acceptable daily intake of PG as 0–1.4 mg kg−1 [6]. To mitigate the potential exposure of PG to individuals, it is crucial to conduct a quantitative assessment of PG concentrations in both food and environment settings.

Recently, a serious of methods for PG detection have been reported, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [8], electrochemical techniques [9], fluorometry [10], colorimetry [11], and other methodologies. Among them, fluorimetry and colorimetry have gained prominence due to their unique advantages, such as rapid response, excellent sensitivity, easy operation and visual output [12]. Therefore, there has been a development of multiple sensors that utilize fluorescence or colorimetry to accurately measure the concentration of target substances. However, many optical detection methods for PG rely on a single colorimetric or fluorometric signal output, which can be affected by various environmental and operational factors [4,13]. This may result in reduced levels of sensitivity, precision, and reliability [14]. In contrast, the development of a dual readout probe that integrates colorimetric and fluorometric techniques can capitalize on the strengths of each method and minimize signal fluctuations through self-calibration, resulting in more reliable and precise analytical outcomes [15]. Therefore, the urgent development of rapid and highly sensitive dual-mode detection for PG is imperative.

Carbon dots (CDs), as emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, have demonstrated significant potential in energy conversion, fluorescence sensing and bioimaging owing to their facile synthesis, good biocompatibility, and exceptional photostability [16–18]. In recent years, researchers have extensively investigated a wide range of precursors, including small molecules, polymers, and biomass, to exploit CDs with various characteristics [19–21]. The initial materials undergo a series of transformations during the carbonization process, resulting in the formation of CDs with a variety of functional groups. To date, there have been several CD-based sensors that demonstrate effective assessment of biomarkers and chemical hazardous substances. For instance, it has been reported that CDs were synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials to detect DA in human serum [22]. Jiao et al. reported the utilization of red fluorescence CDs for the visual detection of metabisulfite and pH through charge transfer and protonation/deprotonation processes involving nitrogen atoms [23]. Xu et al. utilized a cost-effective hydrothermal method to develop CPDs, facilitating fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and TBHQ concentrations [24]. However, there is currently a scarcity of research focused on identifying PG using dual-mode CD-based sensors.

In this study, we present a novel fluorescent and colorimetric CDs-based dual-mode probe (BN-CDs) for the visual detection of PG. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , yellow-fluorescent BN-CDs were prepared using the classic hydrothermal technique with o-phenylenediamine and 5-boronoisophthal as precursors. The prepared BN-CDs sensor displayed exceptional sensitivity and selectivity in monitoring PG through both fluorescence and colorimetry modes. Adding PG resulted in a reduction of BN-CDs fluorescence intensity, likely due to the static quenching effect (SQE). In the meantime, there was an obvious color variation from faint yellow to tawny under sunlight. Additionally, the dual-mode probe was integrated with a smartphone platform, which facilitated on rapid and visual precision detection of PG. Finally, the fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode probe designed for PG demonstrated exceptional sensitivity, specificity, and practicality in detecting real samples. In contrast, this novel dual-mode sensor based on BN-CDs, integrating both fluorescence and colorimetric techniques in a single probe. This dual-signal system not only enhances sensitivity and selectivity, but also improves reliability by offering better self-calibration and resistance to interference, addressing the limitations of previous single-mode sensors. Compared to conventional fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for PG, which rely on a single detection mode, our approach provides a more versatile and user-friendly solution, maintaining simplicity and cost-effectiveness for real-time monitoring applications. This study presents a novel and efficient method for rapid PG detection, enhancing safety in both the food industry and environmental applications.

Proply gallate (PG), tert-butyl hydroquinone (TBHQ), butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA), and butylated hydroxyl toluene (BHT) were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, methanol, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), 5-Boronoisophthal (5-bop) were purchased from Titan Technology Co., (Shanghai, China). The corn oil and peanut oil were purchased from a nearby market, and tap water sourced from this laboratory. The chemical reagents utilized in this study were of analytical grade, and no additional treatments were applied. The experimental procedures involved the utilization of deionized water (with a resistivity of ≥18.2 MΩ cm) obtained from an ultrapure water system. Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, K+, and Na+ were obtained from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Raman spectra were scanned by a LabRAM HR800 spectrometer (Horiba, France). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) analysis was conducted using the Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was employed to acquire X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. The X-ray diffractometer used in this study was D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany). The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected from UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The HITACHI F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) was utilized for conducting the analysis of fluorescent spectra. The fluorescent lifetime analysis was conducted using the LifeSpec II Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The measurement of Zeta potential was conducted using a Zetasizer nano ZS potential analyzer from (Worcestershire, UK).

The successful synthesis of BN-CDs was achieved using a classic hydrothermal technique. In brief, 5-bop (0.42 g) and oPD (0.22 g) were thoroughly dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and then transferred to a hydrothermal reactor, where they were heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was further purified by filtration (0.22 μm filter membrane) and dialysis (500 Da, 24 h) to remove impurities. Finally, the BN-CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying.

The dual-mode detection of PG was implemented by combining fluorescence and colorimetric methods. Specifically, 150 μL of BN-CDs (2 mg/mL) and 100 μL of PG solutions with different concentrations were dispersed in a cuvette with ethanol (2 mL) and incubated for 2 min. After reaching equilibrium, the fluorescence spectra of the probe solution were scanned to record the emission at 530 nm. For colorimetric analysis, the reaction solution was diluted tenfold and its absorption spectrum was recorded via UV–vis spectroscopy. Meanwhile, the probe systems were captured using a smartphone under both daylight and UV lamps (365 nm) after adding various concentrations of PG. The images were then transformed into corresponding RGB chroma values using a Color Picker application, and the linear relationship between RGB values and target concentration was established. Additionally, the selectivity and anti-inference of the dual-mode probe were assessed by introducing several structurally similar compounds and common interferences using identical procedures.

To evaluate the suitability of this dual-mode probe for practical applications, representative samples such as peanut oil, corn oil, and tap water were chosen. The spiked recovery test was conducted by adding varying concentrations of PG (0.5, 10 and 100 μg/mL) to the edible oils and water. The PG-spiked samples (1.0 g) were subsequently mixed with methanol (10 mL) in a test tube and subjected to ultrasound-assisted extraction for 15 min. The supernatant was obtained by centrifuging the mixture at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtration through a 0.22-μm membrane. Finally, the extraction solution was analyzed following the procedure specified in Section 2.2.

The geometry optimization of BN-CDs and its binding product models were performed in the gas phase through B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of theory [25]. The excited-state calculation was performed in an ethanol solvent at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level to simulate the fluorescence spectra [26]. The isosurface maps of frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) were generated in Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) from files exported by Multiwfn.

Here, the dual-mode probe BN-CDs was designed for the detection of PG based on the nucleophilic addition between boric acid groups and PG. Therefore, the BN-CDs were synthesized using the 5-bop and oPD as precursors via classic one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1a). The morphology of the synthesized BN-CDs was initially characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The morphology of the BN-CDs observed in the TEM image was consistently spherical and evenly distributed (Fig. 1 a), with an average particle size of 2.72 ± 0.33 nm (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image of BN-CDs revealed the presence of lattice fringes with dimensions of 0.21 nm, indicating the presence of (100) plane of graphitic carbon [27]. Meanwhile, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and Raman spectrum were also examined to investigate the structure of BN-CDs. The XRD analysis of BN-CDs exhibited a prominent broad peak at 2θ = 21.3°, and no other impurity peaks were observed (Fig. 1c). This peak indicated the presence of amorphous carbon in the BN-CDs, specifically associated with the (002) plane connected to graphitic carbon [28]. As shown in Fig. S1, the Raman spectra revealed the existence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized structure in BN-CDs, as evidenced by two absorption peaks observed at 1576 cm−1 (G band) and 1352 cm−1 (D band). Furthermore, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was examined to elucidate the diverse chemical functional groups present on the surface of BN-CDs. The presence of a wide absorption band at 3410 cm−1 attributed to the N–H bond (Fig. 1d) [29]. Sharp peaks associated with C–N and CC were detected at 1382 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1, respectively [30,31]. Specially, the BN-CDs displayed distinctive absorption peaks at 1349 cm−1 and 1243 cm−1, representing the vibration of B–O and C–B bonds, respectively [32,33]. The findings indicated the existence of boric acid groups on the surface of the BN-CDs. To obtain a more comprehensive understanding, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of BN-CDs were also obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 1e, the XPS spectrum demonstrated the presence of carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and boron (B 1s) within the synthesized nano-probe, thereby providing further evidence for the successful preparation of B, N-doped CDs. In Table S1, the distribution of constituent elements was as follows: C (66.71 %), N (13.32 %), O (12.47 %) and B (7.50 %). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) of BN-CDs was deconvoluted into four prominent peaks, corresponding to C–N (288.1 eV), C–O (287.2 eV), CC/C–C (284.4 eV), and C–B (283.6 eV) bonds [32,34]. The peaks of O 1 s at 531.4 and 533.2 eV were assigned to CO and C–O/C–OH, respectively. Notably, the signal at 532.5 eV corresponding to B–O bond was observed in the O 1s pattern, demonstrating the existence of boric acid functional group (Fig. 1g) [35]. The N 1s spectrum confirmed the presence of pyridine N (400.6 eV) and N–H bond (398.8 eV) (Fig. S2) [36,37]. In the B 1s spectrum of BN-CDs (Fig. 1h), the fitting peaks at 191.4 eV and 192.6 eV were attributed to the binding energies of B–C and B–O, respectively [38]. The outcomes aligned with the observations derived from FT-IR analysis. Overall, the XPS and FT-IR spectra indicated the successful synthesis of BN-CDs co-doped with both B and N elements.

The absorption and emission characteristics were comprehensively investigated to reveal the optical properties of the BN-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the shoulder peak at 345 nm and an obvious absorption band at 450 nm were observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. These spectral features can be attributed to the n - π∗ transition of the C–O/C–N band and the π - π∗ transition of the CC/CO, respectively [39,40]. The faint yellow solution of the BN-CDs exhibited a luminous yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2a inset). The best excitation wavelength of BN-CDs was determined to be approximately 385 nm, which corresponded to the optimal emission center at 530 nm. Additionally, the emission wavelengths of BN-CDs demonstrated consistent stability when excited by different wavelengths, indicating their excitation-independent feature (Fig. 2b). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of BN-CDs was estimated to be 28.47 % using Rhodamine B as a reference (with a QY of 69 %), indicating that BN-CDs can serve as an excellent signal indicator. Moreover, it was worth noting that no discernible alteration in the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs was observed even after continuous irradiation at 365 nm for 60 min (Fig. S3), thus highlighting the remarkable photobleaching resistance of BN-CDs. After being stored for 30 days, the luminescence intensity of BN-CDs remained nearly constant compared to the initial signals, demonstrating exceptional storage stability (Fig. S4). By adjusting the concentration of NaCl and pH, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs showed minimal changes, indicating their excellent fluorescence stability (Fig. S5). The above outstanding features endow them with sufficient capability to function as ideal fluorescent probes.

After verifying the optical properties of the BN-CDs, we investigated the detection feasibility of the prepared probe towards PG. Interesting, the probe solution exhibited significant changes in fluorescence and visible light due to the addition of PG. As shown in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs was significantly reduced in the presence of PG. On the other hand, the addition of PG could lead to the deepening of the yellow solution and increase of the absorbance (Fig. 2d). Therefore, the simultaneous variations in visible light and fluorescence present a viable approach for achieving colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode detection of PG. To enhance the detection performance, it was necessary to optimize certain experimental parameters such as the BN-CDs concentration, reaction time and pH of test solution. As shown in Fig. S6, as the amount of BN-CDs added increased from 10 μL to 150 μL, the fluorescence intensity of the probe gradually decreased and then stabilized. Based on these results, 150 μL was determined to be the optimal amount of BN-CDs for the detection. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the fluorescence and absorbance reached their best response within 2 min upon PG addition and quickly stabilized. Therefore, the optimal detection time for the dual-mode probe to identify PG was set at 2 min. The impact of pH levels on the sensing performance of the BN-CDs dual-mode probe was subsequently assessed. Instead of considering the absolute fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs, the degree of fluorescence change (ΔI) before and after PG addition was taken as the optimization criterion. As shown in Fig. S8, the largest decrease in fluorescence intensity (i.e., maximum ΔI) and the most significant absorbance enhancement were both observed at pH = 7.0. Therefore, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal incubation condition for the dual-mode sensing system. The optimal conditions for response time and incubation pH were determined to be 2 min and pH = 7, respectively.

The sensitivity and analytical performance of the BN-CDs dual-mode probe were evaluated by assessing a range of PG concentrations under the optimal experimental conditions. With the rise in PG concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the BN-CDs probe exhibited a consistent decline, along with a gradual quenching of the yellow fluorescence (Fig. 3 a and b). The fluorescence intensity of the BN-CDs probe demonstrated a strong linear correlation with the logarithm of PG concentration (lnCPG) (R2 = 0.9954), within the range of 0.25–150 μg/mL, as demonstrated by the linear regression equation I530 = −193.29 lnCPG + 1506.3 (Fig. 3c). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.12 μg/mL by employing equation 3δ/K, where δ represents the standard deviation of the blank and K denotes the slope of the calibration curve. In the colorimetric mode, the UV–vis absorption peak of the BN-CDs sensor exhibited a progressive enhancement as the PG concentration increased, accompanied by the deepening of solution (Fig. 3d and e). As depicted in Fig. 3f, a good linear correlation was observed between the absorbance and the logarithm of PG concentration (lnCPG) within the range of 0.5–100 μg/mL. The mathematical representation for this linear relationship can be expressed as follows: A450 = 0.0651 lnCPG + 0.2858 (R2 = 0.9864). The LOD of colorimetric mode reached 0.27 μg/mL, indicating the potential of the sensor for precise and sensitive detection of PG in colorimetry. The designed fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode probe demonstrated significant or even superior performance in terms of linear range and LOD when compared to previously reported sensing approaches (Table S3). Meanwhile, the progressive color changes observed upon increasing concentrations of PG under both ultraviolet light and sunlight facilitated facile visual detection of PG.

Besides, the specificity of the dual-mode probe BN-CDs for PG was assessed by incorporating various common analogues to perform selective testing. The concentration of PG was set at 10 μg/mL, while the analogues were present at a fivefold higher concentration than that of PG. Delightfully, these analogical interfering substances exhibited negligible impact on the performance of the sensor, demonstrating the exceptional selectivity of the designed dual-mode probe for PG detection (Fig. 4 a and b). Meanwhile, the other interfering ions were chosen to explore the anti-interference ability of the dual-mode probe. It was noted that the fluorescence and absorbance changed significantly only in the presence of PG (Fig. 4c and d), suggesting that the dual-mode probe exhibited a high anti-interference property. Furthermore, detection stability is also a crucial criterion for assessing the practical applicability of the dual-mode sensor. The month-long continuous detection of PG demonstrated minimal changes in fluorescence and absorbance (Fig. S9), thereby highlighting the exceptional consistency and reproducibility exhibited by this dual-mode sensor. Overall, the sensor exhibited exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and detection stability, demonstrating great potential for practical applications.

The investigation aimed to explore the potential sensing and reaction mechanisms responsible for the efficient fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs by PG. In general, fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be categorized into fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE), and the inner filter effect (IFE) [41]. Firstly, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of PG was examined, and the fluorescence characteristics of BN-CDs were analyzed. The excitation and emission spectra of BN-CDs did not significantly overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the PG solution (Fig. S10). Simultaneously, the surface states of BN-CDs and BN-CDs@PG were determined by measuring their respective zeta potentials. As shown in Fig. S11, the Zeta potential of BN-CDs decreased from −13.32 mV to −18.48 mV in the presence of PG. These results suggested that the potential for IFE or FRET occurring in the sensing process was excluded [31,42]. The UV–vis absorption of the BN-CDs was subsequently investigated after adding PG. As illustrated in Fig. S12, the introduction of PG resulted in a slight blue shift and an increase in peak width around 450 nm compared to the BN-CDs, which may be attributed to the formation of a new compound [43]. Additionally, the transient fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs were analyzed both prior to and subsequent to the introduction of PG. The fluorescence lifetime of BN-CDs remained largely unaffected by the addition of PG at different concentrations (Fig. 5 a), indicating that the quenching of BN-CDs by PG follows a SQE process. [44]. On the other hand, the Stern-Volmer plot was utilized to obtain deeper insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism.
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
S V
[ M ]

F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the BN-CDs with and without PG, respectively. [M] represents the concentration of PG. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. As shown in Fig. 5b, the quenching of yellow fluorescence at various temperatures (289, 299, 309 K) was completed. The results demonstrated a decrease in Ksv with increasing temperature, providing further evidence for the presence of SQE in the quenching phenomenon of BN-CDs induced by PG [45].

Furthermore, the presence of a SQE phenomenon between BN-CDs and PG was supported by theoretical calculations. This study employed polymerized benzene rings with nitrogen doping to simulate the inner core of the BN-CDs, and the optimized structure models of BN-CDs and BN-CDs@PG were presented in Fig. S13. The photophysical properties of BN-CDs and BN-CDs@PG were computed using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) at the B3LYP/6–31 G (d,p) level with the Gaussian 16 package. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the introduction of PG did not result in any modification to the electron cloud density distribution within the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the reaction product compared with BN-CDs. Meanwhile, the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and HOMO in BN-CDs were found to be −1.81 eV and −3.27 eV, respectively, while those in BN-CDs@PG were changed to −2.02 eV and −3.40 eV. The energy levels and associated energy gaps of BN-CDs@PG were found to exhibit a decrease in comparison to those of BN-CDs. The above results suggested that the decrease in yellow fluorescence observed in BN-CDs could be a result of the quenching process caused by SQE. Additionally, the recognition process of the BN-CDs towards PG was explored through FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra. The comparison of FT-IR spectra for the dual-mode probe before and after PG exposure revealed a novel absorption peak at 1105 cm−1 (Fig. S14), corresponding to C–O–B bonds formation through nucleophilic addition reaction between the boric acid group and phenolic hydroxyl group. Additionally, a significant enhancement in the peak intensity of O 1s was observed in XPS full spectra, which can be attributed to the incorporation of oxygen within PG (Fig. S15). Based on the information provided in the optimization of reaction conditions, it can be inferred that -B-(OH)2 combined with OH− to form -B(OH)3 -. Therefore, the proposed mechanism for the dual-mode response towards PG was depicted in Fig. 5d, according to previous literature and above analysis. Under weakly alkaline and neutral conditions, the boric acid group would exist in the form -B(OH)3 -, and the phenolic hydroxyl group exhibits strong nucleophilicity. Subsequently, the boron atom in the boric acid group serves as the electrophilic center, reacting with the oxygen atom in the phenolic hydroxyl group to form a stable chelate. The results obtained above confirm that PG was recognized by BN-CDs through a process of nucleophilic addition, and the fluorescence quenching observed in the presence of PG can be attributed to SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PG based on smartphone-sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the limited discernment of the human eye in detecting subtle color differences, visual inspection alone cannot facilitate precise analysis of PG. Meanwhile, in order to address the demand for on-site PG analysis in practical applications, a miniaturized detection device has been developed, comprising of a smartphone, a dark box, multiple detection disks, and a BN-CDs probe system. The device was equipped with ultraviolet and sunlight lamps. Various concentration samples can be added to the detection disc, and the fluorescence and colorimetric modes can be freely switched (Fig. S16). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the smartphone-assisted sensing procedure consisted of three steps: sample extraction, image capture, and RGB analysis. In detail, the dual-mode probe was added to a detection disk with different concentrations of PG. After incubating for 2 min, the disk was moved to a dark box, and images reflecting changes in fluorescence or colorimetry were captured with a smartphone camera. The Color Picker software was then used to convert the color information in the images into numerical RGB color model data, enabling the quantitative analysis of PG. The yellow fluorescence intensity in the probe solution visibly diminished upon exposure to UV light, as depicted in Fig. 6b, concomitant with an elevation in PG concentration from 0.5 to 60 μg/mL. A significant linear correlation was found between the logarithm of PG concentration (lnCPG) and the R/(G + B) ratio (y = −0.0702 lnCPG + 0.9121, R2 = 0.9912), with a low LOD of 0.42 μg/mL. Under natural light, the probe demonstrated an obvious color change from light yellow to tawny with the increasement of PG concentrations varying from 0.5 to 60 μg/mL (Fig. 6c). The equation y = 0.1106 lnCPG + 0.7471 (R2 = 0.9942) was established with a LOD of 0.3 μg/mL. The correlations displayed a remarkable resemblance to the pattern observed from both the fluorescence spectrometer and UV–vis spectrometer, respectively. The results strongly suggest the potential of a smartphone-assisted dual-mode probe BN-CDs sensing platform as a promising tool for rapid and cost-effective monitoring of PG through visual changes in color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for detection in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of the BN-CDs under real conditions, the dual-mode sensing platform was employed for quantifying PG levels in both edible oil and tap water. The preprocessed samples exhibited no detectable evidence of PG, thus implementing the spiked recovery test at three distinct concentration levels (0.5, 10, and 100 μg/mL). The average recovery and its relative standard deviation (RSD) were determined based on triplicate measurements conducted at various concentrations. As presented in Table 1 , the dua-mode probe demonstrated satisfactory recoveries (94.0 %–112.0 %) and low relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging from 1.5 % to 3.4 % for both sample categories. The analysis was validated using the standard HPLC method, with the probe results showing good agreement with those obtained by HPLC. Additionally, the accuracy of the smartphone assistant detection platform for PG was evaluated by converting the solution color under UV lamp and sunlight into RGB values. The portable device demonstrated its precise capability in evaluating PG levels in actual samples, with recovery rates ranging from 92.0 % to 113.8 % (Table S4). Overall, the methodology demonstrated significant efficacy in the real-world detection of PG, thereby verifying the applicability and accuracy of the dual-mode probe.

In conclusion, we have successfully prepared and verified a dual-mode probe BN-CDs using fluorescent and colorimetric techniques for the specific detection of propyl gallate. Owing to the presence of boric acid groups, the developed sensing system exhibited exceptional selectivity and high sensitivity. The presence of PG led to a decrease in the yellow fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs, which can be ascribed to the SQE. Meanwhile, the probe solution exhibited a visible tawny color when exposed to sunlight. The dual-mode probe exhibited exceptional sensitivity and selectivity in the detection of PG through both its fluorometric and colorimetric methods. Moreover, the successful integration of the dual-mode sensor with a smartphone has enhanced its efficacy in visually detecting PG by transforming both colors into quantitative data. The satisfying recovery rates were obtained in both edible oil and tap water, demonstrating the practicability of the dual-mode probe in real samples. As a result, the dual-mode sensor could serve as a convenient and effective approach for the accurate detection of PG in food and environment.
Jiangbin Xu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation. Wei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yuanfa Liu: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.128316.
